title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about a sculptor and her unusual approach to her work. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Material Witness
content.subtitle: A magazine profile of sculptor Elena Voss and her unconventional artistic practice.
content.text: Elena Voss's studio is not what you might expect of a prize-winning sculptor. There are no polished surfaces, no carefully arranged tools, no sense of the theatrical self-presentation that often accompanies artistic success. Instead, the large converted warehouse in south London is filled with what most people would call junk: sections of rusted pipe, torn canvas, fragments of demolished buildings, the broken handles of tools. [31] 'I've never bought a material in my life,' Voss told me, without particular pride. 'Everything in here came from somewhere. Everything has already been somewhere.' [/31] || This philosophy — that materials carry histories that can be heard, if not always seen — shapes every decision she makes. Where other sculptors might seek out ideal conditions, Voss deliberately works with imperfection. A crack in a piece of wood is not a flaw to be disguised; it is, she argues, information. [32] 'The material is always telling you about the forces that shaped it. My job isn't to ignore that conversation — it's to join it.' [/32] || Voss came to sculpture late. She studied architecture for four years before abandoning her degree programme, a decision that still surprises people who know how structurally rigorous her work is. [33] But she insists the architectural training was not wasted — it gave her an understanding of weight, load, and balance that most sculptors only develop through years of trial and error. [/33] The difference, she says, is that architecture taught her to work in service of function, while sculpture allowed her to ask what an object was for without needing to answer the question. || Her most celebrated piece, 'Carry' — a large-scale installation made from reclaimed timber beams salvaged from a demolished Victorian warehouse — attracted considerable critical attention when it was exhibited at the Tate Modern four years ago. Critics praised its ambiguity: the beams were arranged so that it was never quite clear whether they were falling or rising, whether the structure was in the process of being built or destroyed. [34] Voss found this critical reading mildly irritating, not because it was wrong, but because it had missed what she considered the simpler and more important point. [/34] The piece was about the weight of work — about the physical labour absorbed invisibly into the buildings we inhabit without ever acknowledging. || When I ask whether she considers herself a political artist, Voss pauses for a long time before answering. [35] 'I think the material is political,' she says finally. 'I'm just the person who puts it somewhere it can be seen.' [/35] || There is something disarming about Voss's lack of apparent ego — unusual in an art world that often rewards those who speak loudest about their own significance. She seems genuinely uninterested in her reputation. What interests her is the next thing she is going to make, and the thing after that. [36] Before I leave, she shows me a pile of reclaimed clay roof tiles she collected last week from a demolition site nearby. She has no plans for them yet, she says. But they are speaking to her. [/36]

questions.question: What does Voss's statement that 'everything has already been somewhere' reveal about her approach?
options: A) She thinks commercial art materials are too expensive to justify buying.
B) She values the past life and origins of the materials she uses.
C) She believes recycled materials are of higher quality than new ones.
D) She is modest about her achievements as a successful sculptor.
questions.explanation: The statement reflects Voss's philosophy that materials 'carry histories', and her comment is explained further as a belief that all materials come from somewhere meaningful.

questions.question: The phrase 'join the conversation' is used to suggest that Voss sees sculpture as
options: A) a way of communicating ideas to a wide audience.
B) a collaborative process between the artist and the material.
C) a means of preserving old materials from being discarded.
D) an opportunity to explain how objects are physically made.
questions.explanation: Voss describes the material as 'telling you about the forces that shaped it', and her role as 'joining' that — implying a two-way creative relationship between artist and material.

questions.question: Why does the writer find Voss's decision to leave her architecture degree surprising?
options: A) Architecture is generally considered a more prestigious career than sculpture.
B) Voss's sculpture shows a level of structural knowledge consistent with architectural training.
C) Completing a degree is normally considered essential for success in the arts.
D) Very few sculptors have any background in engineering or design.
questions.explanation: People are surprised because Voss's work is 'structurally rigorous' — suggesting the architectural knowledge is clearly visible in her sculpture, making the abandonment of the degree seem contradictory.

questions.question: How did Voss react to the critical responses to 'Carry'?
options: A) She was pleased that critics had identified the ambiguity she intended.
B) She was disappointed that the piece had been widely misunderstood.
C) She was somewhat frustrated that reviewers had overlooked her main intention.
D) She was indifferent to the critical reception, as she always is with her work.
questions.explanation: The writer says Voss found the reading 'mildly irritating' not because it was wrong, but because it 'missed what she considered the simpler and more important point' — indicating frustration at an incomplete reading.

questions.question: Voss's response to the question about political art suggests that she
options: A) believes artists have a duty to make explicitly political work.
B) thinks political meaning resides in the materials rather than the artist's intentions.
C) is uncomfortable with the idea that art and politics should be connected.
D) feels her work is too abstract to carry a clear political message.
questions.explanation: Voss says 'the material is political' — placing political meaning in the object itself — and describes herself merely as 'the person who puts it somewhere it can be seen', downplaying her own role.

questions.question: The ending of the article, in which Voss says the tiles are 'speaking to her', is used to show
options: A) that Voss believes objects have supernatural qualities.
B) the contrast between Voss's success and her simple working methods.
C) that Voss's creative process is ongoing and driven by the materials themselves.
D) how Voss plans to develop her practice in a completely new direction.
questions.explanation: The ending echoes Voss's philosophy throughout the piece — that materials communicate — and shows her creative process as open-ended and led by what she finds, not by pre-formed plans.
